title_en: Life Without Screens: This Camp Is a Teen’s Worst Nightmare
title_zh: 没有屏幕的夏天：这是青春期孩子的噩梦

paragraphs.en: Summer. For teens not at work, it’s hot, it’s boring, and it’s an ideal time to close the door and spend about every waking moment watching, playing, texting, streaming—anything but talking—on the phone. With almost half of teenagers in the US saying they’re online almost constantly, the adults in their lives are growing more desperate to drag them off. Families are establishing screen-free zones in their homes, states are banning phones in schools, and a new kind of summer camp has emerged: digital detox camps, which can cost around $2,000 a week, and promise to wean attendees off screens by going cold turkey for the summer.
paragraphs.zh: 夏天。对不工作的青少年来说，这段时间又热又无聊，也是他们最适合把门关上、把每一个清醒时刻都花在手机上——无论是看、玩、发短信、看片，几乎只做不说话的事情。美国几乎一半的青少年都说自己几乎一直在线，周围的大人也越来越想把他们拉离屏幕。家庭开始在家里设立“无屏区”，各州也开始在学校禁带手机，一种新的夏令营应运而生：数字排毒夏令营。它的费用每周大约两千美元，承诺让营员在整个夏天“冷处理”般脱离屏幕，让他们摆脱依赖。

paragraphs.en: WIRED spoke to the founder and director of one such organization. Taking a cue from your average summer camp, the program forces kids to swap their phones and gaming systems for some good old-fashioned social interaction. But in other ways, it’s anything but traditional: It’s staffed with onsite therapists equipped to handle screen addiction, the kids take financial literacy courses, and nearly all campers are completely miserable when they arrive.
paragraphs.zh: 《WIRED》采访了其中一家机构的创始人兼主任。该项目借鉴了传统夏令营的做法，要求孩子们把手机和游戏机换掉，转而进行一些老派的社交互动。但在其他方面，它却完全不传统：营地配备了现场治疗师，专门处理屏幕成瘾问题，孩子们还要上金融理财课程，而且几乎所有营员一到营地就闷闷不乐。

paragraphs.en: Most of the kids who come to our program are very socially stunted. They don’t communicate very well. Everything is in abbreviations. They don’t make eye contact. They can’t finish a full sentence. Everything is mumbled. They don’t want to have an in-person dialog. They would rather do it online or do it through text.
paragraphs.zh: 到我们项目来的大多数孩子，在社交能力上都很受限。他们不太会沟通。对话几乎都用缩写。眼神接触很少。说不出一个完整句子。话都咬在嘴里、不大清楚。他们不愿意进行面对面的交流。更愿意在线上聊天，或者用文字交流。

paragraphs.en: Our camp is about 70 percent boys, 30 percent girls, from ages 13 to 17. Most of the boys are gamers. Most of the girls are addicted to social media—influencer wannabes. None of them want to be there. One kid ran away, and he actually made it down to the freeway, which was very unusual because we’re not close to the freeway. He was picked up by the local highway patrol and brought back. He then went on a hunger strike for three days, and we actually ended up sending him to the hospital because he needed to eat. And then his mom did come and pick him up.
paragraphs.zh: 我们的营地大约有70%的男孩、30%的女孩，年龄从13到17岁。大多数男孩是游戏玩家。大多数女孩则沉迷社交媒体——都想当网红。没有一个孩子是自愿来的。有一个孩子曾试图逃跑，甚至真的跑到了高速公路边，这很不寻常，因为我们离高速公路不近。他后来被当地公路巡逻队拦下带回营地。随后他连续三天绝食，我们最后不得不把他送去医院，因为他需要进食。然后他的妈妈来把他接走了。

paragraphs.en: The line between science and wellness has been blurred beyond recognition. WIRED is here to help.
paragraphs.zh: 科学与健康的边界已经模糊到难以分辨了。WIRED 正在帮你理清。

paragraphs.en: When the kids arrive, we have them unpack to make sure they brought everything they were supposed to bring and that they didn’t bring things they weren’t supposed to bring. Like phones. One kid showed up with three cell phones: When he arrived, he turned in one. We found another cell phone in his bag. And then about three days later his roommate outed him, and we found the third phone. He thought it was funny that he got away with it for that long. That’s most of our kids—if they can stick it to the man, then they’re winning.
paragraphs.zh: 孩子们一到营地，我们会让他们清点行李，确认他们带齐了该带的东西，也没带不该带的东西。比如手机。有个孩子来了三部手机：他刚到时交出了一部。我们又在他包里发现了另一部。大约三天后，他的室友把他“出卖”了，我们又发现了第三部手机。他觉得自己能把事情拖这么久很有意思。大多数孩子都这样想——只要能跟规定对着干一会儿，就觉得自己赢了。

paragraphs.en: Most of the kids are not aggressive, they’re not acting out. More often, they’re moping. But once they come out of their dorm room, we lock the doors. I say, “Sitting in your dorm room moping is not a camp activity.”
paragraphs.zh: 大多数孩子并不攻击性强，也不闹脾气。更多时候，他们只是闷闷不乐。可一旦离开宿舍，我们就会把门锁上。我会说：“在宿舍里闷闷不乐不是营地活动。”

paragraphs.en: Their sleeping and eating habits are horrible. Most kids, especially the online gamers, are up until 2 or 3 in the morning. They don’t get up until noon or later. It’s a disaster. And their eating habits, they’re equally horrible—Doritos and Gatorade, just horrible snack food.
paragraphs.zh: 他们的作息和饮食都很糟糕。大多数孩子，尤其是网游玩家，通常要熬到凌晨2点或3点才睡。他们直到中午甚至更晚才起床。简直是灾难。而且饮食习惯也一样糟——全是多力多滋和佳得乐这种垃圾零食。

paragraphs.en: So we have them on a very specific schedule. They’re in their dorm rooms by 9:30 and have their lights out by 10. And then we wake them up at 6:30. I always tell my staff, “Plan on not sleeping much the first week.”
paragraphs.zh: 因此，我们对他们有一份非常具体的作息安排。晚上9:30他们必须回到宿舍，10点前熄灯。然后我们在6:30把他们叫醒。我总是跟我的员工说："第一周要做好几乎不怎么睡觉的准备。"

paragraphs.en: They have one or two roommates in their dorm rooms. Typically, they are not happy about that. Most kids who come to our program have their own room at home. But what’s funny is that it creates this us-against-them mentality, because they don’t want to be there, and their roommate doesn’t want to be there. They hate us, they hate their parents for sending them there, so they kind of bond without meaning to right off the bat.
paragraphs.zh: 在宿舍里他们会和一到两位室友同住。通常来说，他们对此并不高兴。大多数来我们项目的孩子在家里都有自己的房间。但有趣的是，这反而会形成一种‘我们对他们’的心理，因为他们都不想待在这里，室友也不想待在这里。他们讨厌我们，讨厌把他们送来这里的父母，于是刚开始就不自觉地形成了一种同盟感。

paragraphs.en: We do all the traditional summer camp activities. We take them to the beach once a week. It’s not that much torture. During one of the first years, we had a kid who walked away from the beach day. And he didn’t walk far, but he approached a couple who were taking a selfie and asked if he could use their phone. He ended up calling his mom, saying, “Get me out of here.” His mom did not come and pick him up: The next year, she sent his little brother to camp too. We had more staff members come to beach day after that.
paragraphs.zh: 我们会做所有传统的夏令营活动。每周会带他们去一次海滩。这并不算太折磨。第一年里，我们曾经有个孩子在沙滩日当天从活动现场离开了。他没走远，却走到一对正在拍自拍的夫妻面前，问他们能不能借用手机。结果他最后给妈妈打电话说：“把我接走。”他的妈妈没来接他：隔年，她又把他弟弟也送来营地了。从那以后我们在沙滩日增加了更多工作人员。

paragraphs.en: We also do educational programs and a financial literacy class. We have to break it to these kids that tech companies don’t care about their fun and enjoyment; they’re after their time and their money. We’ve had several kids who have been given credit cards that they then charge up, buying in-app activities or “skins” in games like Fortnite. And then when they can’t spend any more on that card, they’ll use their friend’s card, their mom’s card. We had one kid use their friend’s grandmother’s card. But we never tell a kid, “Never play a video game again, never be online again, never check your email again.” That’s just not the reality of the world we live in.
paragraphs.zh: 我们也会安排一些教育项目和一门金融素养课程。我们要让这些孩子明白，科技公司不在乎他们的娱乐和快乐；他们真正想要的是他们的时间和钱。我们曾遇到好几个孩子，他们拿到一张信用卡后，就开始刷卡购买应用内活动或《堡垒之夜》这类游戏里的“皮肤”。等到这张卡花不出更多钱后，他们就会用朋友的卡、妈妈的卡。有个孩子甚至用了朋友祖母的卡。不过，我们从不告诉孩子“以后再也不玩电子游戏、再也不上网、再也不看邮箱”。那样说并不符合我们所处世界的现实。

paragraphs.en: When we started this program, we didn’t really expect to have returning campers. We figured we’d get you cured, and then next summer you go to surf camp or sail camp or horseback camp or something. But every year we have one or two kids who want to come back—not because they’ve gone back to the dark side of tech addiction; it’s because they want to help the next batch of campers. They want to say to a mopey new camper, “I was you last year. I was exactly where you are, and I turned out OK.”
paragraphs.zh: 当我们启动这个项目时，并没有真的预期会有回营者。我们以为你们会被“治好”，然后明年夏天你就去参加冲浪营、帆船营、骑马营之类的活动。但每年总会有一两个孩子想要回来——不是因为他们又重回了技术成瘾的黑暗一边，而是因为他们想帮助下一批营员。他们想对一个情绪低落的新营员说： “我去年也是这样。我当时和你一模一样，最后我也没事。”
